abstract: In principle, da’wah represents a dedicated effort to convey Islamic teachings to others—regardless of whether they are already believers or have yet to embrace the faith—and covers both individual and collective outreach. This research explores the methodology of da’wah through a specialized analysis of Surah An-Nahl (verses 125–128), utilizing the classical exegesis *Ma’alim al-Tanzil* by Imam al-Baghawi. Employing a qualitative literature-based approach, this study draws upon primary Quranic texts and Al-Baghawi’s commentary, supplemented by secondary data from scholarly journals and historical records. The gathered data were processed using the *tahlily* (analytical) method of interpretation. The findings reveal that Al-Baghawi emphasizes a tripartite framework for effective preaching: the application of *hikmah* (wisdom) rooted in Quranic principles; *maw’izah hasanah* (fair exhortation) through compassionate advice; and *mujadalah* (debate) conducted with grace and intellectual integrity, avoiding hostility toward opponents. Furthermore, the study highlights the ethical boundaries of retribution, advising that responses to opposition must remain just and proportionate rather than excessive.
